data.title: Array Digital Infrastructure: Strong Assets, But The Easy Upside Has Played Out
data.description:
Array Digital Infrastructure, Inc. (AD) is a company that operates under three pillars: a US communications tower portfolio, retained spectrum that is still being monetized, and investment interests that generate cash. AD's towers are mostly located in rural and underserved areas, and have been improving operationally, with 4,572 colocations, with leases to T-Mobile (TMUS), which now account for 46% of tower rental revenue. They completed the $1.0 billion spectrum sale to AT&T (T), plus another $178 million of subsequent T-Mobile transactions expected in 2026. Additionally, AD received $216 million of distributions from unconsolidated entities in 2025. So, overall, AD's prospects are promising, but at the current valuation, I feel my initial bull case has largely played out by now.

Standalone Telecom Towers

Array Digital Infrastructure, Inc. is a Telephone and Data Systems, Inc. (TDS) subsidiary (TDS owns 82% of AD). AD focuses on tower infrastructure, leasing it to tenants and providing related-services. The company was known as UScellular (USM) and changed its name following the sale of the wireless operations to T-Mobile (TMUS) in August 2025. I've previously covered USM and TDS while they were going through their transformative M&A transactions. Today, AD is the entity left standing after USM's deal with TMUS, which is why I thought it was worthwhile reviewing its prospects and updating my thesis.

As a quick overview, AD is technically now a tower company with monetizable frequency spectrum and investment stakes that also generate cash. The company claims to be one of the top 5 tower companies in the US, owning 4,450 towers. AD helps communication providers to deploy 5G wireless and broadband services by leasing its infrastructure. The company also offers support with the logistics of communication networks deployment.

Interestingly, several of AD's towers are located in remote or rural areas, allowing easier coverage in harder-to-reach and underserved places. They cover 19 US states, including the regions in the Pacific Northwest, Northern California, the Midwest, the South-Central U.S., the Carolinas, and New England. AD aims to place multiple tenants and additional equipment in the same tower to increase revenue. Many towers are situated on land that is not owned by the company, and AD is currently prioritizing lower long-term site costs to improve its financial performance.

Similarly, AD now aims to increase its tenant installations or colocations within its tower portfolio. So far, it's been a sharp increase, with 4,572 colocations in December 2025, compared to 2,444 in the same month in 2024. The company still holds some spectrum licenses that it is seeking to monetize. For instance, in January 2026, AD sold $1.018 billion in spectrum assets to AT&T. Other deals are pending, like the one with Verizon, which could generate gross proceeds of $1 billion and is expected to close during 2026.

Operating And Portfolio Update

Aside from these deals, subsequent transactions may include AD's 700 MHz (A Block), 600 MHz, and AWS, with gross proceeds of $178 million, also expected to close this year. The remaining opportunity is mainly their C-Band assets, plus some mmWave and Citizens Broadband Radio Services (CBRS) spectrum. As such, during Q4 2025, AD's tower rental revenues largely depend on TMUS as its largest contributor. Their business with TMUS accounts for 46% of AD's tower rental revenue.

With that in mind, AD's 2025 figures show they generated about $163 million in total operating revenue, up from $102.9 million in 2024. Services also increased to $8.3 million. And their transition arrangement with TMUS currently supports some towers, but once TMUS finishes deciding which sites it actually wants to keep, AD may be left with a meaningful number of towers without a tenant (naked towers). This suggests their revenues may decline if that happens, as the possible range of 800 to 1,800 tenantless towers is significant and is one of the transition's main risks.

Nevertheless, AD is now a leaner operation, which should allow it to focus on finding new tenants for those towers. Still, if some towers are not economically viable, AD may eventually shut them down or remove them. Fortunately, AD also owns investment stakes in other wireless-related entities, which distributed roughly $216 million from unconsolidated entities in 2025. These include $42 million tied to the August 2025 sale of wireless operations to T-Mobile and another $25 million related to Verizon's prepaid lease transaction with Vertical Bridge (another tower infrastructure company). So, AD still benefited indirectly from that deal through its minority investments.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, AD currently trades at a $4.0 billion market cap. Its 2025 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $113.4 million in cash against $674.3 million in long-term debt. Using those figures, I estimate AD's EV at roughly $4.6 billion. And AD is projected to generate $209.3 million in revenues in 2026. This would price the stock at a seemingly high EV/S of 22.0, which is far above its peers' median EV/S of 1.8.

Nevertheless, as I previously mentioned, a lot of AD's value lies in its spectrum assets. The main near-term catalysts combined are worth approximately $1.2 billion in gross proceeds, which should both close later this year. For simplicity, I'll count those as cash additions to AD's balance sheet and recalculate my "adjusted" EV estimate to $3.4 billion. But even then, the implied EV/S is still high at 17.2.

So, to be fair, I'll also account for AD's 2025 distributions from unconsolidated entities ($215.6 million) and interest/dividend income ($18.9 million). And adding those figures results in an "adjusted" EV/S multiple of 8.1, which is much more reasonable, but still relatively high to AD's peers. The good news is that AD is clearly generating substantial cash flows as a standalone entity, and it's bound to have a highly liquid balance sheet in the near term. This does derisk the stock substantially going forward as well.

Consequently, if we also account for AD's latent optionality with its C-band spectrum, it may be enough to justify its valuation. The value for their C-band spectrum is probably the most speculative part for now, and it was a repeated topic during AD's Q4 2025 earnings call. Management appears to be quite optimistic about this retained spectrum, and they're looking for ways to monetize it. It's possible they'll sell it eventually, as it's readily deployable for 5G telecoms.

Unfortunately, it's extremely challenging to give you a reliable estimated value for AD's C-band assets, as it depends on the potential buyers and the available supply dynamics at the time of the deal. But we do know there's a potential regulatory opening for these C-band assets as the FCC's NPRM may expand up to 180 MHz of upper C-band spectrum for terrestrial wireless use cases. So, it's definitely fair to assign some optionality to AD's valuation based on its retained C-band spectrum assets. However, as you can see, there are several assumptions baked into this bull case, and the upside potential is not as obvious to me as it was back in the TDS/USM days.

Conclusion: Neutral For Now

Overall, I think AD is in a great place now post-M&A. The standalone refocus on towers will make it a much leaner operation with steady cash flows and a highly liquid balance sheet. In that sense, I feel my bull case on TDS and USM has largely played out by now. So, AD's prospects going forward are undoubtedly favorable, but at its current valuation, I think there are a lot more variables that need to align to justify its current price tag. This means the risk-reward profile for AD today isn't as clear-cut, which is why I ultimately think it's prudent to rate it a "Hold" this time around.

data.title: The Labor Market Is Flashing Recession Signals
data.description:
This year marks the 18th year that I have been obsessed with the stock market and the economy more broadly. It also marks the 13th year that I have been doing this in a professional capacity. In all that time, I have never predicted that a recession was all but guaranteed. That was until August of last year. I made the claim that, based on current economic conditions, we would see a deterioration in the state of the economy. The end result would be a recession sometime before 2026 was over absent some major shift in policy. The COVID-19 pandemic created significant disruptions. Prices skyrocketed because of those disruptions, as well as because of government stimulus that caused asset price inflation. The Federal Reserve hiked interest rates in order to combat this. And just as the picture was pointing toward a soft landing, tariffs came into the picture, now followed by high oil prices from the war in Iran, which have only made matters worse.

It seems like every week, the data is further supporting my claim from last August. But each time I touch on this topic, my goal is not to rehash old data points. Instead, it is to look at new data, as well as some old data but from a different perspective, in order to get an appropriate picture of what is going on. I would love nothing more than for the US to avoid a recession. But that is looking increasingly unlikely. Headline employment data remains stable, but leading indicators across hiring, wages, and worker behavior suggest that the labor market is weakening beneath the surface.

Workers are struggling

At first glance, it might not seem as though the labor market is going through that much pain. As the chart above illustrates, we have seen unemployment rise over the last couple of years now. However, that has been in large part engineered by the Federal Reserve and its high interest rate policy. The entire objective of higher interest rates is to cause some weakness in the economy in order to pull prices lower. So in that respect, it's not surprising to see that the unemployment rate has gone from 3.4% back in April of 2023 to 4.4% today. For context, we were at historically low unemployment figures. We had not seen an unemployment rate as low as the 3.4% since 1969. And a tight labor market is believed to have some uplifting effect on prices.

Even so, the 4.4% reading that we have today is the highest that we have seen since October of 2021. And if we ignore the COVID-19 pandemic, it is the highest that we have seen since August of 2017. This is not the only employment related indicator that shows some concern though. Consider, if you will, the labor force participation rate. The most recent data point that we have here covers February of this year. The labor force participation rate stood at 62%. Other than what we saw during the COVID-19 pandemic, this is the lowest reading that we have had on record since March of 1977.

Being intellectually honest on this particular metric is necessary. It has long been known that the labor force participation rate as a standalone metric requires significant context. An aging population, combined with a larger share of the labor force deciding to spend more time pursuing higher education, ultimately has led to a long term downtrend since the turn of this century. As the population of this nation grows in age, we should expect some general weakness when it comes to this metric. But still, it is noteworthy just how far it has fallen as of late.

One interesting leading indicator regarding the health of the economy that is almost never touched on is the temporary help services employment data. This measures the number of employees who are employed on a temporary basis in the US. The most recent data that we have here covers through February of 2026. During that month, there were 2.45 million individuals working in this capacity. That was down 3.4% from the 2.53 million that we saw just a year earlier. And again, if we ignore the COVID-19 pandemic, it's actually the lowest reading that we have had since January of 2012.

As the chart above illustrates, it actually has a tendency to decrease right before we enter into a recession. And that decrease accelerates throughout the economic downturn. I do find it peculiar that this time around it is down substantially, and yet a recession has not officially started. Just because a metric has been accurate in the past does not mean it will be accurate in the future. So this is not a guaranteed assessment of what is going on. But when combined with the other data points, and considering its long term consistency leading up to this, it is definitely concerning.

Job openings data is also pointing in a disturbing direction. In the chart above, you can see that while it is around where it was prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, the overall trend has been worsening for a couple of years now. When companies experience or are anticipating difficult times, the number of job openings naturally declines. And the 6.95 million that we had in January of this year was one of the lowest readings that we have had in the last decade. The data below, meanwhile, shows that non-farm hiring data is coming in worse and worse as time goes on.

The number of hires amounted to 3.3% of all jobs in January of this year. We have seen a few other months in the last year or so where that number has been similarly low. But if you strip out the 2025 and 2026 window of time from the equation, this would be the lowest rating that we have had since October of 2013. It also happens to be below the 3.9% that was recorded a year ago. Also disconcerting is the fact that, as you can see in the chart below, the number of non-farm quits is also depressed. While it has been lower, such as in December of 2024, as well as September and October of 2025, those are all fairly recent periods. To find a reading lower outside of this short window and outside of the pandemic, you would have to go back to July 2015.

The issues extend beyond just jobs

In other articles, I have talked about challenges that average Americans face. We are dealing with a cost-of-living crisis. But in addition to this, changes in how the economy is structured have resulted in a smaller and smaller share of productivity flowing to the masses. Instead, it is disproportionately going to the wealthiest Americans. And contrary to what some believe, trickle-down economics is a fiction.

As the chart above illustrates, the share of income going to labor in the US non-farm business sector has been falling for decades now. It is especially low at this point in time, with the third quarter of 2025 registering the lowest point on record. Some of this is the consequence of technological change. A larger and larger share of the economy is being driven today by software, automation capabilities, and the like. That is destined to push the share of income that goes to labor lower absent a change in tax policy in the US. But there have been other structural contributors as well. For instance, union participation is at multi-decade lows. Back in the 1950s, somewhere around 35% of all workers we're part of a union. Today, that number is closer to 10%. Unions have a great track record, not only in the US, but in other nations, of negotiating higher wages. In fact, unions are so powerful and prevalent in some countries that they have essentially established significant minimum wage floors without needing a government mandated minimum wage. Examples here would be Denmark, Sweden, Norway, and Iceland. As union membership declines, workers lose bargaining power.

Globalization has also been a driver here. As the US began importing goods from other countries like China, Taiwan, and other nations, manufacturing here at home, which had historically offered attractive wages, weakened considerably. Competing with low-cost foreign labor is difficult. And then when you add on top of this other things like major companies that control large chunks of their respective markets, it's not hard to understand the challenges facing labor.

It's no coincidence, I would argue, that consumer confidence levels are literally near all time lows. In the chart above, you can see consumer confidence data conveyed by the Federal Reserve. In the chart below, meanwhile, you can see something disturbing. The consumer confidence data goes back to 1977. That is 49 years. And yet, 15 of the worst months for consumer confidence have come since the COVID-19 pandemic ended. Ten of those, or 40% of the 25 worst months, have happened in either 2025 or 2026. For context, the 2008 and 2009 financial crisis years saw a combined five months in this list of the 25 worst.

We are seeing some other concerning data points as well. In the charts below, you can see year over year wage growth from late 1997 through February of this year. The 4% increase that we saw in February matched what was seen in December of last year and in January of this year. However, to get a reading this low prior to that time, you would have to go back to January of 2022. What's more, as the subsequent chart below illustrates, all four quartiles of workers have seen year over year weakness in job growth. The highest quartile ended up seeing the greatest impact, which would be the highest paid members of the workforce. In February of 2025, their year over year increase was 4.9%. But in February of this year, it was 3.9%. If even the highest paid workers are seeing resistance when it comes to pay increases, that is most certainly telling of broader economic distress.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my assertion that the economy is cracking even further. The labor market is struggling now more than at any point in the last several years if we ignore the brief window that was the COVID-19 pandemic. Consumer confidence levels are abysmal, and jobs data is pointing towards further weakening moving forward. When combined with my findings from other articles, I have no choice but to call this economy rocky. Contrary to what some might think, the labor market does not usually collapse overnight. Instead, it weakens quietly, with lower hiring, reduced wage growth, and declining worker and consumer confidence, slowly but surely emerging in headline results. The current environment increasingly resembles that early stage.

data.title: TotalEnergies: Nearing Or At A Cyclic Peak (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The Euro Oils have just killed it since the first of the year. After lying comatose for all of 2025, the New Year dawned and they've rallied 26-52%. What went right for these intrepid E&P's? Primarily the oil price began to rally after the Maduro extraction and tilted sharply higher with the on-set of operation Epic Fury. Traders have been staying up late, hunched over their terminals, sipping strong coffee and generally clutching their pearls non-stop ever since.

One of things that may be driving the stock of TotalEnergies, (TTE) and the other members of this select group is booked reserves. For the first time in forever people are wanting the security that having a few billion barrels of the gooey stuff. If that is the case, then the upgrade from TD Cowen's, Jason Gabelman is easily understood. Along with a cash flow waterfall coming when key projects come on line, single-digit production growth, and superior returns on capital employed, Gabelman commented-

Moreover, peer performance suggests investors have begun to value resource life more fully than in the recent past, perhaps as a result of plateauing U.S. shale production growth and decelerating non-OPEC growth, Gabelman said.

This is something I discussed in my last bullish article on the company, pointing it out as a particular strong point for TTE. This performance has been continued in 2025 with reserves to production replacement of 120% with reserves life of 12+ years.

Another thing I pointed out in my last article was TTE's success in growing organically through exploration in recent years, and avoiding the expensive mega mergers larger competitors, ExxonMobil, (XOM) and Chevron, (CVX) have undertaken. This enables a very competitive cost structure that keeps capital pointed toward the bit, and free cash generation pointed toward shareholders.

We've done pretty well with this recommendation-up ~50%, but let's be honest, the last several months of this rally are the result of the conflict with Iran. I've avoided making prognostications about when this will wrap up. One thing I have been clear on is I expect a reset-above previous levels-the game has changed and reverberations of the current volatility aren't going away anytime soon, but substantially below current levels. TTE remains a great investment, but there is a short term trade on the table. I would carefully consider taking some profits on this rally with the idea of buying back in after a 15-20% reset. I am putting a sell on TTE at current levels.

A quick word on the upstream energy sector macro

We are coming out of the consolidation phase of the cycle. Upstream companies have spent credit, capital and stock merging with rivals to reduce their cost structure and enhance their portfolios. Now costs are rising, onshore and offshore. A new exploration cycle is beginning, and capex will be increasing as companies try to maintain output. The easy money has been made in the Euro upstream E&P's for the cycle. Over the last several years PE multiples have risen 100-300%. (Don't get me started on shale companies, PE multiples up 4-5X for the same period. The sector is richly valued. This is not to say we are headed for a precipice. As yet. Upstream equities may continue at current or slightly higher levels for another couple of years, depending on global events. But with higher input costs, and increasing capex all that's needed for a reset is...you guessed it, lower crude prices, perhaps brought on by demand destruction. My view is that it's time to start positioning for the reset.

The investment case for TTE

Total is a global energy giant with a diverse geographic portfolio. Oil production, LNG, chemicals, and refining drive their operations. Nearly all their 2025 revenue was tied to oil and gas and derivatives sales, with only about 1% coming from Integrated Power, their renewables division. During the year, TTE raised BOEPD 4% to 2,545 mm, and as the graphic below shows has a diverse portfolio of project startups to maintain output this modest growth profile for 2026. It's base projections for cash flow of $70 Brent and $12.50 Mbtu TTF are conservative as well.

TTE also performs at the top of curve on the key metrics below. To my eye, the most critical is the success TTE has in replacing production and maintaining reserves. This speaks to the quality of their assets, and it bears pointing out the company has nearly the same proved reserves life as XOM, a company roughly 3X their size.

The company maintains a low gearing ratio of 15% as compared with some of the other Euro oils-BP, (BP)-25.7%, Shell, (SHEL), 19%, and Equinor, (EQNR) at 22%. This also corresponds to the company's sector leading ROACE of 12.8%.

TTE projects a significant reduction in investment capital-capex in 2026 that combined with increasing cash from operations-CFFO, will enable growth in free cash by $10.00 bn.

Finally it's hard not to admire the company's view of its dividend obligations. You don't often see dividend growth referred to as "Sacrosanct" in company filings."

A catalyst for TTE-The Orange Basin-Africa

West Africa has emerged as star in the continent's energy growth story. And, no one has a better resource base in the Namibia play than TTE. With substantial reserves already booked and two projects approaching FID, Venus-2026, and Mopane-2028, TTE has the potential to enter the early 2030's with 350-400K BOEPD from Namibia concessions. The Orange Basin extends into South African waters, and TTE has future exploration projects identified for both countries. In short TTE has a long runway in this basin.

A step away from offshore wind in the U.S.

It should come as no secret that the winds have shifted regarding offshore wind in this country. The federal government has taken active steps to discourage and deny permitting for these projects, including some under construction and nearing completion. Some stakeholders like Equinor, (EQNR) have chosen to fight the government in court. In the case of the Empire Wind project, the fight has proven worthwhile. So far.

TTE has taken a more practical route in my view. Just announced is their agreement with the Dept. of the Interior to surrender their leases offshore the U.S. East Coast, recovering their lease fees as part of the deal. In a statement following the conclusion of this transaction, the company noted that this technology was unnecessary and expensive in the U.S., and there were other options for power generation. TotalEnergies Chairman and CEO, Patrick Pouyanné also discussed the company's plan to pivot with the proceeds to the surging U.S. LNG sector-

These agreements, under which we will reinvest the refunded lease fees to finance the construction of the Rio Grande LNG plant and the development of our oil and gas activities, allow us to support the development of U.S. gas production and export.

I think TTE made a good decision here. Investing in the start up, Rio Grande LNG-which we discussed recently here, just makes sense. It takes capital tied up in a fraught industry and moves to one growing exponentially. Fitting into an LNG portfolio that already represents a big chunk of TTE's international footprint, Rio Grande gives it access to a low cost future stream of export revenue headed toward the lucrative TTF market in the EU.

Risks to our thesis

Two things could hamper the company's near term plans and revenues. The first is its exposure to Qatar's North Field East LNG, start up for which the company was expecting its share-6.25% of the projects 2 BCF/D output. This facility was damaged in a recent missile attack by Iran, taking about 17% of the projects capacity offline for at least several years.

The sell thesis is predicated on the expectation for a substantial decline in crude prices with the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. While a substantial operation appears to be taking shape to do this, the outcome without U.S. casualties-for which the American electorate has absolutely zero appetite, is far from certain. Taking this step could be a flashpoint for this conflict, in which case crude baskets could easily double in prices, and derail the sell thesis for TTE.

Several industry execs noted at CERAweek in Houston that there is unrealized dislocation building in the global energy trade-

"You can't just take 8M-10M bbl/day of oil and 20% or so of the [liquefied natural gas] market off the world stage without having some significant repercussions," ConocoPhillips (COP) CEO Ryan Lance told CERAWeek attendees.

The physical supply of oil is much tighter than prices in the futures market suggest, Chevron (CVX) CEO Mike Wirth said. "There are very real, physical manifestations of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that are working their way around the world and through the system that I don't think are fully priced into the futures curves on oil."

Your takeaway

The upstream sector has been propelled higher in the face of the Iran conflict's creation of the expectation of supply interruptions, and the real possibility of the damage and destruction to energy infrastructure in the Middle East. The entire Euro cohort began a rally in early January, with a sharp inflection higher in March. If that isn't a war premium boost, I don't know what one is.

Balanced against the expectation for a fall in crude prices post the Iran conflict are the lucrative crude prices now extant. Guesstimates for TTE's earnings in Q-1, have risen from $1.60 to 1.88 per share. So has the stock price forecast of a high at $102.00, with a median of $94.00, reflecting trader's suspicions that higher prices may be sticky for the foreseeable future.

The company has tried to get ahead of trader's concerns about supply and distribution by commenting that increased prices will more than offset the 15% reduction in its current output. TTE CEO, Patrick Pouyanne commented in this regard during an interview at CERAWeek in Houston-

The Brent market is ok, but the products market, which is the one which impacts customers … is much higher than Brent,” he told CNBC at S&P Global’s CERAWeek energy conference in Houston. TotalEnergies is a major player in the global LNG market, including the largest exporter of U.S. LNG. The CEO said the company can still fulfill customer orders in Europe and Asia thanks to its diversified global portfolio.

So there you have the up and the down side of the current war-impacted energy conundrum. Things could go higher, or they could go lower, making investment decisions at current levels look like genius moves or just the opposite. I suppose it comes down to your risk tolerance.

A price drop into the middle-$60's would satisfy my fairly low threshold for risk tolerance and I would then rerate them as a strong buy. As for now, my recommendation if you are fortunate enough to be long, is to place a tight stop to capture profits. If history is any guide, everything that was old-meaning lower prices, will be new again.

data.title: Palantir Is Now U.S. Military's Critical System
data.description:
I bought Palantir (PLTR) stock in May of 2024 at roughly $21 a share. In those days PLTR was on a bull run of its own with solid retail interest and strong momentum. I took it as a momentum trade. It worked. I rode it to about $82 by December of 2025.

But then the stock had its first major drawdown. It started tanking, and I did not have an investment thesis at the time, because I took it as momentum trade. A momentum trade gives you no reason to hold through volatility. So I waited, waited some more, and then eventually exited my entire position by January 2025 around $67.

We all know what happened next. Palantir went on a tear toward the $200s and I watched the whole show from the outside. Although I made solid returns on the initial trade, but I could've made much more if I had held even 20% of my position.

Not having an understanding of the company I was in and not having a long-term investment thesis around it, cost me this much. After selling, I just watched for months. The one thing that kept me from re-entering was that PLTR looked too expensive. They needed to prove the valuation was worth having. And by late 2025 September, October it started to prove that.

The stock was trading around the $170s, but I still couldn't convince myself to buy at the highs. But I have this thing where I cannot bring myself to buy at all-time highs when a stock is in price discovery mode. So I started selling OTM (out of the money) cash secured puts, roughly 3 months out, about 20% to 25% below the market price at the time. The premiums were paying me between $10 to $15 per share which is $1000 to $1500 per contract just to wait and buy PLTR at a price you want.

I sold these put contracts twice. The first time was 100 days out and collected about $11 per share in premium. The contract expired worthless because Palantir never touched the strike. And I just pocketed the premium. The second time I collected about $15 per share and on last Friday the contract expired in the -money. My strike was $145 and PLTR closed at $143. I was praying for this to happen.

Over 200 days I have collected about $26 per share in premium just to wait and buy PLTR at a better price. Now I own 100 shares at $145, but after netting out the premiums my effective cost basis is about $119 per share.

And this time, I have a thesis. So let's get to it.

Their Execution Changed My Mind

The main reason I waited so long to re-enter Palantir is that I wanted the company to prove that their sky-high valuation is worth it.

Palantir's revenue for Q4 of FY25 was up 70% Y/Y to $1.41 billion. That is the highest quarterly growth rate they have ever reported. For the entire year, revenue came in at about $4.48 billion, up 56% Y/Y. And the growth is accelerating, which is rare for a company of this size. Many SaaS companies start to decelerate well before they hit $4 billion in annual revenue. They are going the opposite way. And there is a reason for that. More on that later below.

Their total US revenue in Q4 was $1.076 billion, the first time it topped a billion dollars in a single quarter. Within that US commercial revenue grew 137% YoY to $507 million, while US government revenue grew 66% to $570 million.

Now, this revenue mix is quite notable. For the past couple of years, the bear case (And my opinion too) on Palantir was that it was too dependent on government contracts. But this time, that old argument is getting harder to defend. Palantir's US commercial business is now growing at more than double the rate of its government business. The old narrative is basically gone.

On the profitability side, things are even more impressive now. Operating income for the quarter was up YoY to $575 million, which is a 41% GAAP operating margin. Also, net income came in at about $609 million with a 43% net margin, which is much higher than the roughly 10% net margins in the same quarter a year ago. Palantir ended the quarter with $7.2 billion in cash and US Treasuries with basically zero debt.

They went from barely breaking even on an operating basis to generating over half a billion dollars in operating profit in a single quarter. I think that things are getting much better now for them, and now they are kind of proving their valuation. But don’t get me wrong, Palantir is still quite expensive to own, but there are reasons for that too, which I will discuss later below.

They have solid cash flow margins. A 51% free cash flow margin means for every dollar of revenue, more than $0.50 converts directly to cash. Which is very rare for high growth SaaS companies. Because usually high growth SaaS companies burn cash for adoption and growth. Palantir's unit economics are much better than most SaaS companies.

Maven Infrastructure

Deputy Secretary of Defense Steve Feinberg signed a memo directing Palantir's Maven Smart System to be designated an official program-of-record across all branches of the US military, to be formalized before the end of FY26.

For those who are not familiar with how the Pentagon's budgeting works, a program-of-record means Maven will get its own line in the defense budget. That line item is protected. It means the Maven system is not a program that future administrations could easily cancel. It will become permanent infrastructure with funding across every branch of the military.

This designation came out after Palantir's Maven was at the center of Operation Epic Fury from the very first hour it launched. Within the first 24 hours of strikes the platform reportedly processed over 1000 targeting recommendations. Since then, the Pentagon has conducted thousands of strikes through the platform.

Now it is an operational system that is proving in real-time that the technology works. I believe this is the most consequential proof point in Palantir's history. And I don't think that's an overstatement. Because they have an AI system that was tested in an actual multi domain military operation and processed thousands of targeting packages and delivered results at a speed and precision that no human workflow can match. Once a system starts delivering like this it basically becomes irreplaceable.

On the business side, the Maven contract has grown from a $480 million award in 2024 to a $1.3 billion by May 2025. And the Pentagon is reportedly about to spend $13.4 billion on AI advancements. Maven is now one of the primary platforms that will absorb a good chunk of that allocation.

Palantir's US government segment is currently running at about $2.28 billion in annualized revenue (based on the Q4 run rate of $570 million). If Maven adoption expands to all military branches as mandated by the program of record designation and the Pentagon spends its $13.4 billion annual AI budget, then Palantir's US government ARR could easily get from $2.28 billion today to somewhere in the range of $3.5 to $4.5 billion by FY28. This is roughly double the current run rate over two to three years. And this could be from the government's side alone.

The program-of-record designation basically guarantees a baseline level of spending that will not go away. But it will depend on how quickly the other branches adopt Maven and how much of that $13.4 billion AI budget ends up being funneled through Palantir's platform.

In my opinion $4.5 billion might even be conservative when you are a platform that the Pentagon is actively conducting operations through. And also the program of record status makes this revenue politically durable.

Iran Conflict Is The Real World Proof

Now, as I was writing this, I realized that discussing military conflict as an investment opportunity might feel uncomfortable to many of you. But unfortunately this is the world we live in; we constantly face new threats that we need to be able to deter and defend against.

Right now Iran is sending swarms of kamikaze drones all around the Middle East and terrorizing the world. We need technological superiority to defend against these kinds of threats. We need autonomous AI systems that can track these waves of targets and defend against them. The reality is that Palantir is being used in live military operations, and what happens there directly impacts the company's strategic position, contracts, and revenue.

As I was writing this piece, The Washington Post reported that the Pentagon is developing plans for potential ground operations in Iran, including possible raids by Special Operations forces and infantry troops. The missions under consideration include the seizure of Kharg Island and raids into coastal areas near the Strait of Hormuz to destroy weapons targeting commercial shipping.

Officials estimated these objectives could take "weeks" to complete. For Palantir it's a proving ground. Every strike processed through Maven that results in a successful operation, every intelligence analysis that proves accurate—all of it builds a case for deeper integration.

And from everything we can see, Maven is working. Alex Karp confirmed on CNBC on March 12 that the system, including Anthropic's Claude, is actively being used in Iran operations. I expect that once this conflict reaches its conclusion or at least a phase that allows for operational reviews we may see after action reports that may credit AI platforms like Maven for operational outcomes.

Ontology Moat

This is the part I didn't understand when I first bought Palantir and sold too early. Palantir has spent over two decades doing something that was boring, but now, due to AI, every major organization desperately needs to do it. If they want to implement AI on an enterprise level.

That is building a data ontology across an enterprise. What that means is that basically they take all the data sources within an organization and map them to entities and events (basically specialized databases). Every object in the ontology is linked directly to data sources, which are continuously updated.

Now, once the ontology is in place, building new applications, automations and AI agents on top of it becomes very easy and scalable. In the past, companies would usually do this because if they had a huge collection of data and they wanted it to be organized so that if the dataset got larger, it wouldn't create a mess.

And keep in mind that it is hard, labor-intensive work in the initial deployment cleaning the data, mapping it, and defining the objects. But once that foundation is done, the cost of adding new use cases is very low. Now every organization has to do this due to AI. Because if these companies want their productivity to 10x using AI then they must have all their data clean and organized in a dataset that could be fed to AI so that the AI agents can execute real world tasks based on the data. Otherwise, what are you going to tell the AI agent to do? (Claude, increase my sales by 100%, make no mistakes.) Obviously not.

This is why Palantir's incremental YoY normalized profit after tax margins are running at close to 70%. The ontology is the moat. It's the reason their customers spend 39% more annually (139% net dollar retention).

I think this ontology first approach positions them uniquely within the agentic AI market which is projected to grow to $196 billion by 2034 at a 43% CAGR. Agentic AI systems that autonomously reason, plan and act require exactly the kind of structured datasets that Palantir has been building.

Most enterprises that want to deploy AI agents are going to know that their data isn't ready. Palantir's value proposition is making that data ready.

Valuation Is A Problem

Even after all these arguments above, I cannot defend Palantir’s valuation; it is quite expensive to own. If you want to own it you need to accept that it is expensive for the reasons I explained above. Because the market knows the importance of it and has priced it accordingly, but also prices it for perfect execution too.

Which makes it risky in the sense that if the sentiment goes negative around it, then it can have big drawdowns too. PLTR’s chart speaks for itself.

At around $143 a share, Palantir trades at about 190x trailing non-GAAP P/E and 108x forward non-GAAP P/E.

By any valuation metric it is expensive. Their EV/Sales ratio is 2311% above the sector median. But if you want to see why the company is expensive then look at the PEG ratio because on a trailing basis, it has PEG ratio is 0.98 which means you are paying just 1x for each unit of growth.

Anything below 1 on a PEG basis means the stock is actually fairly valued relative to its growth rate. At 56% revenue growth, 231% EPS growth, and expanding margins, I think that the growth denominator is doing heavy lifting.

If Palantir hits the midpoint of its FY26 guidance, $7.19 billion in revenue with $4.13 billion in adjusted operating income. And then if we apply a 23% effective tax rate to the adjusted operating income, we get roughly $3.18 billion in adjusted after tax earnings. At roughly 2.57 billion diluted shares, that's $1.24 in adjusted EPS.

At the current price of $143 this results in a forward P/E of about 125x on FY26 estimated adjusted earnings. Still expensive but if we project forward to FY28 using the government ARR growth I modeled ($3.5 to $4.5 billion) and commercial revenue trajectory (115%+ guided growth) total revenue could reasonably reach $12 to $14 billion by FY28.

At a 55% adjusted operating margin, that's $6.6 to $7.7 billion in adjusted operating income, or roughly $5.1 to $5.9 billion after tax which translates to $2 to $2.3 in EPS. So you are paying about 67 to 78x FY28 earnings for a company that would be growing revenue at 40%+ and generating $4+ billion in free cash flow. And the platform that runs the US military's AI operations is penetrating the enterprise AI market at 137% growth. I would argue that valuation risk is worth taking.

Anthropic Phase-Out Problem

There is one more thing (risk) that I want you guys to be aware of other than valuation. On March 3, the Pentagon designated Anthropic as a supply chain risk after a dispute over guardrails on military use, specifically around autonomous weapons and surveillance.

Anthropic's Claude powers some of Maven's most critical features. As of mid-March Karp confirmed on CNBC that Claude is still actively powering Maven during operations in Iran.

President Trump also directed all federal agencies to stop using Anthropic's technology with a 6 month phase-out period. Defense contractors like Lockheed Martin have already mandated that operations cease using Claude. Palantir must certify it doesn't use Anthropic models in Pentagon work once the transition period ends.

The problem is that the code architecture that calls Claude versus OpenAI's models is much different, especially when you factor in context, caching, memory handling and output parsing. Across customers and mission critical military deployments, that's a huge amount of re-engineering work.

Karp said that Maven will be designed to work with multiple AI models rather than depending on any single one. The question is how long the transition takes and whether it creates temporary operational disruptions or not.

But I believe that model independent Maven would actually be more politically durable and more attractive to allied governments that may have their own restrictions on specific AI providers. If Palantir transitions to a multi-model architecture, in my opinion it would be a net positive in the long term for them.

My Position and Final Thoughts

I own 100 shares of PLTR at an effective cost basis of $119. I intend to hold this position for at least 3 to 5 years and may add to it on pullbacks using the same put selling strategy. They are building the data infrastructure that makes AI actually useful in complex real world environments. Maven is proving its value in live combat. I am okay with paying a premium for a company that is probably mission critical to the world's most powerful military and growing its commercial business at 137%.

data.title: Ameren: A Good Safe Haven Play, But Valuation Looks Questionable
data.description:
Ameren Corporation (AEE) is a regulated electric and natural gas utility that primarily serves residential and business customers who are located in the U.S. states of Missouri and Illinois. Ameren is a relatively large utility, especially when compared to some of the other utility companies that we have discussed over the past several months. This comes, at least partially, from the fact that Ameren serves some of the largest cities in each of the two states. For example, Ameren serves the city of St. Louis, Missouri, which is the second-largest city in Missouri. More importantly, however, Ameren possesses many of the characteristics that have made utility companies some of the most common holdings among conservative, risk-averse investors. The company also managed to achieve significant growth over the course of 2025, which is also something that many investors will appreciate. Finally, Ameren has a very reasonable valuation at the current price. These factors combined might make it a decent holding for anyone who is looking to ride through the current market volatility.

About Ameren Corporation

As mentioned in the introduction, Ameren Corporation is a regulated electric and natural gas utility that serves customers in the U.S. states of Missouri and Illinois. The company's service territory is quite large, as it includes most of the Greater St. Louis, Missouri, area, as well as most of Illinois, other than the city of Chicago:

While significant portions of both Missouri and Illinois are rural, with Illinois alone having 75% of its total land area dedicated to farms, there are still quite a lot of individuals living within Ameren's service territory. In fact, Ameren has approximately 2.5 million electric customers and 900,000 natural gas utility customers as of December 31, 2025. These customers are almost evenly split between Missouri and Illinois, particularly in the electric space:

State

Electric Customers

Natural Gas Customers

Missouri

1.3 million

100,000

Illinois

1.2 million

800,000

This is actually somewhat nice because it limits Ameren's exposure to a single state's regulatory regime. For example, Illinois is a so-called "deregulated" state in which multiple suppliers produce and sell electricity (although transmission and distribution are still regulated by the authorities). Missouri, on the other hand, is not a deregulated state for electricity, and customers located within Ameren's service territory have to purchase their electricity at the price approved by the Missouri state regulator.

It is somewhat questionable whether regulated or deregulated states are better for utility company profits. The original impetus of the push for deregulation back in the 1990s was that by allowing customers to choose their electricity provider, competition would be put into the markets, and this competition would push down consumer electricity prices. However, as an article published on Real Clear Energy points out:

If state lawmakers truly want to lower electric bills, pushing deregulation is a mistake. Historically, this policy has raised electric bills. In fact, customers living in deregulated states have paid an extra $19 billion dollars more for electricity.

The Real Clear Energy article cites an article that was published in the Wall Street Journal as evidence for this claim. For its part, Ameren's electric utility business in Missouri has substantially higher revenue than its business in Illinois:

State

FY 2025 Revenue

Missouri Electric Operating Revenues

$4,631

Illinois Electric Operating Revenue

$3,261

As we can see, the electric revenue that Ameren brings in from its electric utility in Missouri is substantially higher than the revenue that it brings in from its electric utility in Illinois. This is not necessarily a perfect comparison that we can actually use to compare the two markets in terms of how much the different regulatory structures affect the company's performance. This is because Ameren is a delivery-only utility in Illinois. The company does not own any generation capacity in Illinois. It merely purchases electricity from the Illinois Power Agency and delivers it to some of its customers. The company's most recent 10-K states that Ameren purchased power on behalf of 28% of its customers in Illinois during 2025. This is different from Missouri, as the company actually generates its own electricity in Missouri and sells it to its customers. This means that Ameren is not actually making any money from the electricity itself in Illinois; the company is only earning distribution and transmission fees. In Missouri, Ameren is earning the distribution and transmission fees just like in Illinois, but it also receives the generation fees from the sale of the electricity itself. Thus, it makes sense that the company would have higher revenues in Missouri, given the very similar customer counts.

One way in which regulators determine the price that a utility company, such as Ameren, is allowed to charge its customers is by allowing it to earn a specific return on equity. As Enerdynamics explains:

Return on equity, also known as ROE or the cost of equity capital, describes the return on the equity portion of the rate base that regulated utilities are allowed to collect in rates.

The article goes on to further state that regulators try to allow the utility to provide a high enough rate of return that they can actually attract investors to purchase their equity, but not so high that the people who depend on the utility for their electricity and natural gas are being overcharged. It is a balancing act, and it is certainly not an exact science, but investors should still be aware of it in order to make better decisions about which utility to purchase.

Perhaps unfortunately for investors, different states have different policies for the return on equity that a utility is allowed to earn. This is another reason why Ameren's electric utility customer base being split between Missouri and Illinois is a nice thing. The company does not have to be completely exposed to the policies of a single regulator. Ameren's allowed return on equity in Illinois varies by business unit:

Ameren Business Unit

Allowed ROE

Ameren Transmission

10.48%

Ameren Electric Distribution

8.72%

Ameren Illinois Natural Gas

9.60%

Unfortunately, the company has not been able to provide an allowed return on equity for its operations in Missouri as of the time of writing. The 10-K states that new rates became effective in June and September 2025, respectively, but the order allowing the rates did not specify an allowed return on equity:

As such, it is difficult to compare the regulatory regimes between the two states. Regardless, however, it is a pretty fair conclusion that having exposure to two states and two separate regulatory regimes provides Ameren with a diversification advantage over an electric utility that is solely exposed to a single regulatory authority.

Stability and Recent Growth

As mentioned in the introduction, Ameren has many of the characteristics that have historically made utilities reasonably popular investments among risk-averse types, as well as made them somewhat useful as a safe haven during challenging economic periods. One of these characteristics is that the company exhibits a great deal of stability regardless of macroeconomic conditions. We can see this in Ameren's revenue, which has grown from $5.909 billion in 2017 to $8.470 billion for the full-year 2025 period:

(All figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

There were a few challenging economic periods over that nine-year period. The COVID-19 pandemic and the shutdown of the economy may be the first to come to mind, as that resulted in a surge in the unemployment rate due to many businesses shutting down (either temporarily or permanently), and naturally, unemployed people frequently struggle to pay their bills. At the end of 2020, the total amount of unpaid electric and gas utility bills was approximately $32 billion, with some estimates stating that about 20% of households were behind on their utility payments at the time. As we can see in the chart above, however, Ameren was not really affected much by this, as its full-year 2020 revenue only showed a slight decline compared to 2019 levels. This alone is a testament to the company's stability in challenging macroeconomic conditions.

The pandemic of 2020 was not the only challenging period during the timeframe shown above, however. In 2022, crude oil and gasoline prices spiked along with interest rates. The American Psychological Association reports that due to increases in prices throughout the economy that year, the majority of American adults reported that financial problems were causing them stress, and 57% said that they did not have enough money to cover their basic needs. Admittedly, these results were obtained from a survey that the organization conducted, so some individuals might question the validity of these numbers, but during that same year, there were also a number of mainstream news stories discussing how rising prices throughout the economy were forcing people to make difficult decisions about skipping meals in order to pay the electric bill. As such, it seems likely that there were many people who were financially struggling during 2022. Despite this, we can see that Ameren's revenues came in much stronger in 2022 than they did in 2021. This provides further support for the idea that the company enjoys relatively stable financial performance regardless of economic conditions.

This could be a desirable characteristic for a company, given the current economic backdrop. As we all know, oil prices have surged over the past few weeks due to an outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East and the largely successful efforts of Iran to control shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, the average price of gasoline in the United States was $2.936 per gallon in January and $3.039 per gallon in February. AAA states that the average price of gasoline in the United States was $3.93 per gallon last week. That is a fairly large increase in a product that many people use a significant amount of in a relatively short period of time. As more than half of the population of the United States claims to be living paycheck to paycheck, we can conclude that the surge in gasoline prices will cause financial difficulties for many people. Rabobank actually goes so far as to state that the surge in crude oil prices might cause a recession if high gasoline prices force enough people to cut back on consumer discretionary spending. That would obviously be a bad thing for any company that is dependent on the willingness and ability of consumers to spend. However, a company like Ameren should hold up much better since most people will prioritize paying their utility bills ahead of most discretionary purchases, and the company has a history of weathering challenging macroeconomic conditions without too much difficulty. We can see that clearly in its historical trailing revenues.

Revenue is not the only measure of financial performance that shows stability and growth for Ameren. The company's net income exhibits remarkable stability regardless of economic conditions:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

We immediately notice that there was not a single year over the entire nine-year period in which Ameren's net income declined. In each of the years, the company's reported net income came in stronger than it did in the previous year. This was even the case in 2020, with the economic shutdown of the pandemic and the surge in utility bill delinquencies still unable to cause Ameren's net income to decline. This further strengthens the case that Ameren could be a very good investment for someone who is worried about the possibility of a near-term recession.

Over the nine-year period shown, Ameren managed to grow its net income at a 12.05% compound annual growth rate. This is far greater than the rate of inflation if inflation is measured by the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers. According to that index, $523 million in January 2017 has the same buying power as $697.91 million in December 2025. Ameren's net income for the 2025 period was $1.456 billion, so obviously it managed to deliver earnings growth that was higher than can simply be explained by inflation. This is very nice to see from the perspective of an income investor. After all, as we have discussed before, one of the biggest goals of an income investor is to ensure that their money lasts for the remainder of their life. In order to do that, they need to be invested in companies and securities that manage to maintain their purchasing power over the long term. Ameren's net income appears to be doing that.

However, one of the biggest problems with utilities is that they need to keep making capital investments into their infrastructure in order to maintain their rate base. As mentioned earlier, regulators typically allow a utility company to earn a specific rate of return. The rate base is the value of the company's assets that is used for this calculation. As the value of the company's assets decreases each year due to depreciation, the utility needs to continually invest more and more money into its rate base in order to keep the value steady, let alone increase it. Thus, a utility company consistently has high capital expenditures, and it needs to raise money from investors to finance these capital expenditures. This is accomplished through the issuance of both debt and equity, the latter of which has the effect of steadily diluting the existing shareholders. As such, a utility company's earnings per share will normally rise more slowly than its net income. This is the case for Ameren, which we can see by looking at the company's earnings per share over the past nine years:

As shown, Ameren's earnings per share went from $2.16 per share in 2017 to $5.38 per share in 2025. This is only a 10.67% compound annual growth rate, or less than the company's net income growth over the same period. However, it is still more than sufficient to beat inflation. The Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers implies that $2.16 in January 2017 had the same purchasing power as $2.88 in December 2025. Obviously, Ameren Corporation managed to do quite a bit better than that. Thus, it does appear that the company has done a pretty good job of protecting investors against inflation over the nine-year period.

Naturally, as investors, we are not only interested in how well a company has done in the past, but we are also interested in what it will do in the future. Fortunately for potential investors, Ameren appears likely to continue on its historical growth trajectory. The primary way that it will do this is by growing its rate base. As we have already discussed, regulators typically allow a utility company to earn a specific return on equity, so the higher its rate base, the more dollars that specified percentage will be. Alongside its most recent earnings results, Ameren announced its capital spending plan for the 2026 to 2030 period:

Over the five-year period from 2026 to 2030, Ameren plans to invest $31.8 billion into utility infrastructure upgrades, modernization, and capacity growth. We can see that the bulk of this spending is allocated for the Missouri business as opposed to the Illinois business. This may be surprising given that the company has very similar customer counts in each state, but it becomes more understandable when we consider that Ameren is a delivery-only utility in Illinois. In Missouri, the company actually generates the electricity that it sells, and constructing generation capacity adds to the total capital expenditures needed to serve that particular state. As of right now, Ameren has around 2.6 gigawatts of new generation capacity under construction in the state of Missouri:

Energy Center

Generation Capacity

Anticipated In-Service Date

Bowling Green

50 MW

Q1 2026

Split Rail

300 MW

Q2 2026

Castle Bluff

800 MW

Q4 2027

Big Hollow BESS

400 MW

Q2 2028

Big Hollow CTG

800 MW

Q3 2028

Reform

250 MW

Q4 2028

This new capacity is scheduled to come online between now and the end of 2028, as we can see in the chart. The company's capital spending plan states that it will be spending about $14.5 billion on new generation capacity in Missouri between 2026 and 2030, which is approximately 46% of all of the company's planned capital investments over the period. The company does not, however, state whether or not the above generation plants are the only ones that it intends to construct by 2030 or if more could be in the pipeline. When we consider the need to construct generation capacity in Missouri, the fact that this state is receiving the bulk of the company's capital spending is understandable. If we subtract out the money budgeted for new generation, we see that only about $6.8 billion is being devoted to the company's non-generation infrastructure in Missouri, which is not too far off the numbers for Illinois.

As presented, Ameren's capital spending program should grow its rate base at a 10.6% compound annual growth rate through 2030:

However, it may be unrealistic to expect the company's earnings per share to grow at the same 10.6% compound annual growth rate over the period. After all, as was already mentioned, Ameren will need to raise capital to finance this growth, and part of that financing strategy will be the issuance of new shares. This will dilute the investors and prevent the company's earnings per share from growing as rapidly as either the rate base or net income. Ameren's management projects that, given the current financing needs, the company's earnings per share will only grow by about 6% to 8% over the 2026 to 2030 period. If we assume that the company's price-to-earnings ratio remains stable over the same period, then investors can likely expect the share price to increase at a similar pace. In addition to the share price appreciation, investors will also receive the dividend that the company pays out, which is currently 2.76% of the share price. This overall gives us a projected total return of 9% to 11% annually for the next five years, which is not bad at all for a utility company. However, it is also not exceptional, as most utility companies aim to deliver an average total annual return of around that level.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed Ameren Corporation was on March 16, 2025. Thus, just over a year has passed since we last looked at it, and as we all know, there are a lot of things that can occur in a year's time. This past year was no exception, as only a few weeks after my last article was published, the United States shocked the market by imposing a series of tariffs on most of its trading partners. This even led to fears that there would be a recession by the end of 2025. However, ultimately, things calmed down, and there was no recession. The market also regained all of the losses that it suffered in the aftermath of the tariff shock, and the S&P 500 Index (SP500) finished 2025 at very close to an all-time high.

The markets have been much more somber this year, however. As I mentioned in a recent article, one of the biggest reasons for this is that investors have begun to doubt whether or not generative artificial intelligence technology will truly be as profitable as they had previously estimated. The nation's largest technology companies are spending substantial amounts of money to construct data centers to support the technology, but as of yet, these same companies have not presented credible monetization strategies for the technology. The S&P 500 Index (SPY) is down 6.17% year-to-date, but that is almost entirely due to the technology companies that represent an outsized proportion of the index. The Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), which equally weights all constituents of the index, has held up much better:

This indicates that much of the market weakness has been centered around the technology sector and, specifically, the giant technology companies that dominate the market-capitalization-weighted index.

Of course, as I mentioned earlier, the recent surge in oil prices has caused some market watchers and analysts to become concerned about a recession in the near term. Indeed, it is very possible that an extended elevation in oil prices will strain the budgets of ordinary people and cause a sufficiently large decline in consumer spending to push the economy into a recession. However, according to Moody's Analytics, about half of U.S. consumer spending comes from the wealthiest 10% of households right now, and this is the group that is least likely to be adversely affected by rising oil prices, so it is possible that rising oil prices will not have as much of an impact on consumer spending as some analysts fear. As a result, it is possible that the United States will avoid a recession, but that does not mean that families who are lower on the income and wealth scale will not have to cut back in the face of rising energy prices.

As we have already discussed, though, Ameren is a regulated business that provides a product that most people consider to be a necessity. As such, they tend to pay their utility bills even when money gets tight. This shows up in the company's finances, as they have generally proven to be quite stable regardless of the broader macroeconomic conditions. It also shows up in Ameren's stock price, which has risen by 7.93% since the last time that we discussed the company:

Unfortunately, we can see that the company's stock price underperformed the S&P 500 Index over the period, but that is not atypical, as utility companies frequently do underperform the high-growth stocks that dominate the large capitalization index.

Interestingly, Ameren has actually outperformed the S&P 500 Index significantly since the start of this year:

Ameren's share price has risen year-to-date, which is a rarity right now. After all, as anyone watching the markets can testify, most asset classes have fallen since the start of this year. The fact that Ameren's share price has actually risen helps support the thesis that this is a "safe haven" company that may be able to help investors ride out the turbulence plaguing the rest of the market.

Valuation

According to Zacks Investment Research, Ameren will grow its earnings per share at a 9.27% rate over the next three to five years. This is quite a bit higher than the company's own management projects, but it is reasonably consistent with what Ameren has delivered over the past nine years. In fact, this projection is lower than the EPS growth that Ameren has delivered since 2017. As such, it is not unreasonable, but it might be a bit optimistic.

The Zacks EPS growth rate projection gives Ameren's stock a 2.21 price-to-earnings growth ratio at the current price. Here is how that compares to some of the company's peers:

Company Name

Current PEG Ratio

Ameren Corporation

2.21

Entergy Corporation (ETR)

2.17

DTE Energy (DTE)

2.65

Exelon Corporation (EXC)

2.81

CMS Energy (CMS)

2.70

FirstEnergy Corp. (FE)

2.63

Eversource Energy (ES)

2.94

This suggests that Ameren Corporation is currently very reasonably priced compared to other utility companies. However, we should note that the above PEG ratios were calculated using the Zacks earnings projection. If instead we were to use the 7% midpoint that the company's management has projected, then Ameren's price-to-earnings growth ratio comes in at 2.92, which makes it somewhat expensive compared to its peers. As such, we might want to look at other valuation measures before making a determination about the company's valuation.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Valuation System assigns Ameren a valuation grade of "D" at the current price, noting that it is more expensive than the utility sector average on a variety of metrics:

Here is how the same system rates the company's peers:

Company Name

Seeking Alpha Quant Valuation Grade

Ameren Corporation

D

Entergy Corporation

D-

DTE Energy

C-

Exelon Corporation

C+

CMS Energy

D+

FirstEnergy Corp.

B-

Eversource Energy

B+

This system certainly suggests that Ameren is somewhat expensive at its current price. The only company shown in the chart that has a less attractive valuation is Entergy, with all of the others comparing somewhat better with the peer median overall.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Ameren has appeal as a "safe haven" investment in today's volatile market. The company has a history of weathering through challenging economic conditions with ease, and its recent stock price performance has reflected this. After all, Ameren is one of the few companies in the market that has actually delivered share price gains this year. The company provides a necessary product, and as such, its customers will likely continue to pay their bills to Ameren even if the economy worsens due to surging oil prices. The only problem with Ameren right now is that the valuation is questionable. While the stock appears to be very reasonably priced when using certain earnings projections, I am concerned that these projections are a bit overly optimistic. As such, for now, I will maintain my "hold" rating on Ameren, but this stock becomes a buy if the stock price declines to under $100 or so (which would put its forward price-to-earnings right around the utility sector median).

data.title: APOS: Back To A Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

The Apollo Global Management, Inc. NT 7.625% 53 (APOS) is an issue that I last covered over two years ago (article link). While the Note does not mature until 2053, Apollo has the right to Call it every five years starting in 2028. As part of my analysis, I found two other Notes with similar features as I deem that important when deciding if a single issue is a good investment choice.

My rating in 2024 for APOS was a Sell, down from a Buy due to the price jump during that time period. I currently see no risk in terms of payment or bankruptcy concerning APOS as its coverage ratios are excellent, plus backstopped by Apollo Global. With its 7.5% yield, I am again giving it a Buy rating.

Apollo Global Management Review

Seeking Alpha describes Apollo Global Management (APO) as (edited):

Apollo Global Management, Inc. is a private equity firm specializing in investments in credit, private equity, infrastructure, secondaries and real estate markets. The firm prefers to invest in private and public markets. The firm's private equity investments include traditional buyouts, recapitalization, distressed buyouts and debt investments in real estate, corporate partner buyouts, distressed asset, corporate carve-outs, middle market, growth, venture capital, turnaround, bridge, corporate restructuring, special situation, acquisition, and industry consolidation transactions. For credit strategies, the firm focuses to invest in multi-sector credit, semi-liquid credit, direct lending, first lien, unitranche, whole loans and private credit. The firm launches and manages hedge funds for its clients. It also manages real estate funds and private equity funds for its clients. The firm invests in the fixed income and alternative investment markets across the globe. The firm conducts in-house research to create its investment portfolio. Apollo Global Management, Inc. was founded in 1990.

This is how Apollo Global Management describes themselves:

At Apollo, we ask what if to create what is, because yesterday’s strategies may not solve tomorrow’s challenges. The answer? Rigorous thinking and innovative solutions for an ever-changing world.

We seek to provide flexible financing to help companies adapt, evolve and lead. We help institutions achieve long-term financial goals through investment strategies designed to deliver strong risk-adjusted returns and help individuals build lasting wealth.

We invest alongside our clients with purpose and precision, drawing on deep expertise across Credit, Equity, and Real Assets, with a sharp focus on private investment grade and ﬁxed income strategies. Our platform spans markets and geographies, bringing together deep origination capabilities and the ability to invest across market cycles—so we can meet our clients where they are.

Source: Apollo homepage.

The next diagram shows Apollo's structure.

Apollo Global believes the investment world is changing and is adjusting their portfolio based on their outlook, as shown on the following chart.

I use two measures to gauge the risk of owning a Note: interest payment coverage and Par value coverage. Looking at the Income statement, we see that the Net Income is 77X the $45MM annual payments to APOS holders.

Turning to the balance sheet, the Total Common Equity (TCE) is 36X the $600MM Par value of the Notes. When including the Minority interest, that ratio jumps to over 70X. Also note how much the TCE has climbed in 2020.

Understanding APOS

Important features include:

  • The Notes are Callable after five years but do not mature until 2053.
  • The initial coupon is 7.625%. On 9/15/2028 and every five years thereafter, the new coupon will be equal to the 5-yr TSY rate + 3.226%.
  • Current ratings are A3/BBB. Combining this with the coupon reset rule, investors are getting 3% extra yield for owning a Note with a low investment-grade rating versus a 5-yr TSY Note with high investment-grade rating.
  • The Issuer may, on one or more occasions, defer interest payments on the notes for one or more optional deferral periods of up to five consecutive years without giving rise to an Event of Default under the terms of the notes. All deferred interest must eventually be paid.
  • The Notes rank below senior debt, equal with other subordinated debt, and above all classes of stock.

Conclusion

I currently see no risk in terms of payment or bankruptcy concerning APOS as its coverage ratios are excellent, plus backstopped by Apollo Global. With its 7.5% yield, I am again giving it a Buy rating. I'm in the process of reviewing FGN but a quick look at its coverage ratios does not justify the extra yield it currently offers.

Portfolio strategy

Long-date debt should see price improvements if the entire rate curve drops as the FOMC reduces the Fed Funds Rate, or FFR. The best time to add such debt naturally is at the bottom of the rate cycle when the investor can “lock in” the best YTC and/or YTM. That said, if the rate can reset, as APOS does, guessing where rates will be at the next reset date becomes "fun." I could not find a BBB-rate forecast; what is shown is the 5-YR UST forecast, so the slope is what matters, not the level.

When picking between issues that float, I look at two components:

  • Floating rate used: The two most common are the 3-month SOFR and the 5-Yr UST. Logic says that the second normally will be higher than the first due to time difference.
  • Fixed component used: Stating the obvious, the higher the better. Looked at in another way, this would be the minimum coupon if the floating rate part drops to zero.
